description: A17FJ - Day 1: Earth Orbit and Translunar Injection

tokens: Apollo 17 has just entered an orbit around Earth after a successful and spectacular night launch that was 2 hours, 40 minutes late due to a ground problem.
Very shortly, the spacecraft will go out of range of the tracking ship USNS Vanguard.
Had it launched on the originally intended azimuth of 72°, it would have passed trough the reception area of a station on one of the Canary Island.
Instead, the actual azimuth of 91.503° will take it far south of that station.
It will next be picked up by the Carnarvon tracking station in Australia half an orbit around Earth.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 000:13:47 -This is Apollo Control; coming up on 14 minutes after lift-off, that lift-off coming about 2 hours, 40 minutes late and we'll be assessing the effects of that late lift-off on subsequent events in the mission timeline, passing those along.
One of the effects will be a change in the Acquisition Of Signal/Loss Of Signal time as we move - move along on the ground track.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 000:16:42 -This is Apollo Control at 16 minutes, 45 seconds after lift-off.
We've confirmed Apollo 17 is in a near nominal orbit.

The crew reported an orbit of about 93.5 by 89.5 [nautical miles, 173.2 by 165.8 km] based on their onboard calculations, and computations on the ground show that we're very close to the nominal 90-mile - nautical mile [166.7 km] orbit.
As a result of the late lift-off, the Translunar Injection will be a little bit earlier than the Flight Plan Ground Elapsed Time.
We don't have an update on this time yet.
We expect that it will be on the order of 8 to 10 minutes early.
We'll update that time as we get a later update.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 000:17:39 -We would expect - expect that the time of arrival at the Moon will be approximately the same as the Flight Plan time in terms of Greenwich Mean Time.

The Ground Elapsed Time will be somewhat earlier, and we expect that there will be clock update - a so called clock update - at some point where we make the clocks in Mission Control and aboard the spacecraft agree with the Ground Elapsed Time that they would be showing in the Flight Plan.

The net effect will be that we'll arrive at the Moon in a shorter Ground Elapsed Time - in effect about 2 hours, 40 minutes earlier than the Flight Plan would show, but at the same Greenwich Mean Time or local time here on Earth, that we would have had, had we lifted off on time.
We're in effect making up the time by speeding up the arrival at the Moon.
The spacecraft at Translunar Injection will be going somewhat faster than a nominal lift-off Translunar Injection.
Consequently, it will arrive at the Moon going slightly faster, and also somewhat earlier, about 2 hours, 40 minutes earlier in terms of Ground Elapsed Time.
This will also mean that the Lunar Orbit Insertion will require a bit more energy to slow the spacecraft down and get it into lunar orbit.
These details of course, will all be worked out in the time that we have before our Lunar Orbit Insertion.
And, when we get an updated Translunar Injection time, we'll pass that along.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 000:23:56 -This is Apollo Control at 24 minutes.

Apollo 17 now in an orbit about 90 [nautical] miles by 93 [nautical] miles [167 by 172 km], and everything appears to be nominal aboard the spacecraft and aboard the launch vehicle, Saturn third stage.
One additional impact of our late lift-off will be the loss of television coverage for the transposition and docking maneuver.
The television coverage will not be possible because the ground track has shifted and we don't have the site coverage that had been expected for television.

The Translunar Injection burn, reigniting the Saturn third stage to put the spacecraft on its trajectory to the Moon, is now scheduled to occur at a Ground Elapsed Time of 3 hours, 12 minutes, 35 seconds; or roughly 9 minutes earlier than the Flight Plan time.
This again the effect of the late lift-off.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Cernan, from1973 Technical debrief:"Launching at night, we just had a somewhat different view of the Earth than most other flights have had.

The first real view we got of being in orbit at that point was pretty spectacular because it happened to be Earth sunrise and that's a very intriguing and interesting way to get your first indoctrination to Earth orbit."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Schmitt, from1973 Technical debrief:"The transcript contains some descriptions, by all three of us, of sequences of that sunrise which, in the color banding, may be of some significance for other people."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Schmitt, from1973 Technical debrief:"I think the tapes cover some comments on the Earth illumination at the horizons.
Briefly, right at the terminator horizon of the Earth, you get sharp shadow definition of cloud features.
At the sunlit horizon from lunar distance that's a very clear definition between the black of space and the upper portion of the Earth.
In Earth orbit and near Earth, you can see the gradation of that horizon caused by the atmosphere.
At night around the Earth, there's a very clear horizon glow all around the Earth.
Air glow, I guess you would call it.

And the horns of the crescent Earth are much sharper and elongated compared to those of the crescent Moon, as if light was being diffracted into the atmosphere and in extending the length of the horns of the crescent."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 000:25:11 -And we'll be reacquiring Apollo 17 through the Carnarvon Tracking Station at a Ground Elapsed Time of about 52 minutes, 20 seconds; roughly 27 minutes from now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: By having reached 50 statute miles in altitude, Ron and Jack qualify for their astronaut wings.
This was the definition of space by the US Air Force at the time.
More recently, the definition has been internationally agreed at 100 kilometres (62.137 statute miles, 53.996 nautical miles or 328,084 feet)
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: 000:32:02 -This is Apollo Control.
Now 32 minutes after the lift-off of Apollo 17.
We have Loss Of Signal with the spacecraft.
We'll be reacquiring through the Carnarvon Tracking Station in about 20 minutes.
And from the President of the United States we have the following message to the crew of Apollo 17.
The message reads: "As you set forth on the final Apollo expedition to the Moon, I want to have my personal best wishes for a successful mission and safe return.

I am sure your voyage of scientific exploration will be the crowning achievement in a program which has expanded Man's horizons, brought great credit to your country and lifted the spirits of people all over the world.
Godspeed to you all." Signed Richard Nixon.
The Flight Dynamics Officer, continuing to process tracking data following orbital insertion, reports that there is a small amount of out of plane error showing up in the orbit.
This it is believed is due to a small error in the Instrument Unit of the Saturn third stage.
However, the orbit is very close to nominal; about 90 nautical miles by 93 nautical miles [167 by 172 km].
And we look very good, both with respect to the spacecraft and with respect to the Saturn third stage which must perform that burn, putting Apollo 17 on its trajectory toward the Moon.
At 34 minutes, this is Apollo Control Houston.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 000:35:33 -This is Apollo Control.
The postlaunch press conference at Cape Kennedy is scheduled to begin at 12:33 Central Standard Time, 1:33 am Eastern Standard Time.
Again, that time 12:33 Central Standard Time, 1:33 Eastern Standard for the post-launch press conference at Cape Kennedy.

tokens: 000:51:00 -...Control at 51 minutes into the flight of Apollo 17 and we're standing by to reacquire the spacecraft through the Carnarvon, Australian tracking station.
One of the things the Booster engineer will be looking for when we reacquire and get good lock on the data will be the Saturn third stage Instrument Unit.
Looking at one brief bit of data before we lost signal, it appeared that one of the four batteries in the Instrument Unit had a very high current drain on it.
We will be looking closely at that to see if it was simply a telemetry problem or if, in fact, that battery does have some problem.
And we should be about 15 seconds now from reacquiring.

tokens: 001:25:00 -This is Apollo Control; 1 hour, 25 minutes after lift-off.
During the post-launch press conference at Cape Kennedy, we had a short acquisition with the crew through the Hawaiian tracking station.
During that period of conversation, and during that period of monitoring the systems on the spacecraft and the launch vehicle, we found that the situation was essentially unchanged.
That is, both vehicles looking good; the spacecraft and the launch vehicle.
And we're progressing toward a normal Translunar Injection 1 hour, 46 minutes, 50 seconds from now.
The crew has discussed one unexplained series of events.

It appears that when certain switches are cycled or moved on panel 2, which is the main panel in front of them, the center panel of the spacecraft, they're getting a master caution and warning signal.
A light comes on - a tone comes on.
This is to attract the crews attention that something may be wrong and the normal procedure is then to look at another matrix of lights which would zero them in on the problem.
The light - the system or the subsystem or particular area being monitored, which had the problem would light an individual light.
However, when they go to this other matrix of lights, they find that none of them are lighted.
This is leading the crew and flight controllers here in Mission Control to believe that they're getting an spurious signal to the master caution and warning, when in fact nothing is wrong.
We don't have any further explanation for the problem at this point.
We will continue to look at the data and particularly during the translunar coast, we think we'll get a good long time to look at things in detail and try to find out precisely what is happening.
At this point however, the problem presents no concern and one of the more likely explanations, or possible explanations that's been advanced is perhaps some contamination in some switches.
We did accumulate some taped conversation during the press conference with the crew.
We'll replay that for you now and then stand by for Acquisition Of Signal over the United States.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: 17, Houston.
We're back with you.

tokens: Cernan, from1973 Technical debrief:"By the time we came back over the States on the first pass, we were ready and the spacecraft was ready, and we were configured and could have gone on a TLI-0 without any hurrying and scurrying whatsoever.

From that point on, when we got our Go on the booster and a Go for TLI-1, it was an Earth-orbit, an extra Earth-orbit ride to sit back and just monitor our systems in the spacecraft and see what we could see from Earth orbit in terms of viewing.
It was an extra 90 minutes of the flight that, if you really had to do without, you could have.
And it was not hurried.
It was very comfortable, even progressing toward the TLI-0."
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: By 'TLI-0', Gene means that he felt they could have headed for the Moon with a Translunar Injection burn without having completed a full orbit of Earth.

We had one note here.
I didn't even remember until I read it here that in the ECS checks.
The hydrogen pressure indicators, or part of the indicators, were reading about 10 percent lower than we expected.
But, as I recall, it may have been expected."

tokens: Contamination inside switches had proved to be a serious problem on previous flights.
On Apollo 14, just before landing, an intermittent short circuit inside the LM's Abort switch had to be worked around.

Apollo 15's CM had a Thrust switch for the SPS engine that, upon inspection back on Earth, was found to have a tiny strand of wire floating about inside that was likely the cause of a short circuit in space.
Although the switches are X-rayed prior to flight, small items of contamination can be hidden behind the metal parts of the switch itself.

tokens: Often during the flight, Mission Control will read up lists of important information, mostly numerical, that the crew will use for some function, be it firing engines, taking photographs or some other task.
These are known as Pre-Advisory Data, or PAD for short in the acronym-rich world of NASA.

Every planned manoeuvre is accompanied by one of these PADs but in addition to that, Mission Control will ensure that, for all stages of the flight, the crew will have a contingency PAD to hand with which they would be able to get home without help in an emergency.
The first PAD about to be read up is a contingency PAD for a possible abort with an ignition time of 90 minutes after Translunar Injection.
After that there will be another PAD which is fully intended for use, for it has the details of the Translunar Injection burn itself.

tokens: Having managed to get the last part of the TLI plus 90 PAD to Jack, Overmyer has added another abort PAD, this of a different type that uses P37.

First of all, an interpretation of the TLI plus 90 PAD follows:Purpose:This PAD provides the details of a long burn of theSPSengine that would occur about 90 minutes afterTLIin case the crew need to make an emergency return to Earth soon after heading for the Moon.Systems:The burn would be made using the largeSPSengine at the rear of the Service Module, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.CSM Weight (Noun 47):66,953 pounds (30,369 kg).Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):-0.59° and +1.88°.
These angles represent an initial direction for the gimbal-mounted engine.

As the burn progresses, the nozzle will be slowly steered to track shifts in the stack's centre of mass.Time of ignition (Noun 33):4 hours, 40 minutes, 1.48 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, -351.8 (-107.2); Y, -0.1 (-0.03); Z, +3,378.2 (+1,029.7).
The change in velocity is resolved into three components which are quoted relative to the local vertical frame of reference.Spacecraft attitude:Roll, 180°; Pitch, 73°; Yaw, 3°.

The desired spacecraft attitude is measured relative to the alignment of the guidance platform.HA, expected apogee of resulting orbit (Noun 44):Not applicable.HP, expected perigee of resulting orbit (Noun 44):20.1 nautical miles (37.2 km).
The perigee distance is so low, it intersects Earth's atmosphere.
What this really means is that the spacecraft will re-enter.Delta-VT:3,396.4 fps (1,035.2 m/s).

This is the total change in velocity the spacecraft would experience and is a vector sum of the three components given above.Burn duration or burn time:4 minutes, 50 seconds.Delta-VC:3,380.8 fps (1,030.5 m/s).
Using its ability to independently measure acceleration, the EMS can shut down the engine in case the G&N system fails to do so.

This figure, Delta-VC, is entered into the Delta-V counter (hence 'C') and is slightly lower than Delta-VTbecause the EMS does not take account of the engine's tail-off thrust.Sextant star:Star 11 (Aldebaran, Alpha Tauri) visible in sextant when shaft and trunnion angles are 342.4° and 32.3° respectively.
This is part of an attitude check.Boresight star:Not available.

This is a second attitude check which is made by sighting on another celestial object with the COAS (Crew Optical Alignment Sight) at a window.The next five parameters all relate to re-entry, during which an important milestone is "Entry Interface," defined as being 400,000 feet (121.92 km) altitude.

In this context, a more important milestone is when atmospheric drag on the spacecraft imparts a deceleration of 0.05 g.Expected splashdown point (Noun 61):13.29° north, 32.0° west; in the Mid-Atlantic Ocean west of the Cape Verde Islands.Range to go at the 0.05 g event:1,099.2 nautical miles.
To set up their EMS (Entry Monitor System) before re-entry, the crew need to know the expected distance the CM would travel from the 0.05 g event to landing.

This figure will be decremented by the EMS based on signals from its own accelerometer.Expected velocity at the 0.05 g event:34,904 fps.Predicted GET of 0.05 g event:24 hours, 38 minutes, 9 seconds GET.GDC Align stars:Also known as the set stars, these are used in the event of a primary guidance failure to align the spacecraft's backup attitude system.
This system is based around Body-Mounted Attitude Gyros (BMAGs) and Gyro Display Couplers (GDCs).
The spacecraft would be rotated to cause stars Sirius and Rigel to be viewed through the telescope in a particular manner.

It would then be in a known attitude with respect to the current reference and a set of alignment angles can be entered into the GDCs to give them a starting point.GDC Align angles:Roll, 318°; pitch, 148°; yaw, 358°.The one additional note in the PAD is that there would be no need to perform an ullage burn to settle the contents of the SPS tanks because they are full.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The TLI plus 90 PAD gives a complete description of the burn to which it refers.
However, the crew has another means of defining a return-home burn which uses Program 37.
Only four items are required and the computer will work out the other details.
An important condition for P37 is that the spacecraft must still be in Earth's sphere of influence, thus simplifying the calculations.

The program takes the four values from the PAD that Overmyer read up:Time of ignition:9 hours GET.Change in velocity, fps (m/s):4,897 fps [1,493 m/s].Longitude of the splashdown:175° west.Start of re-entry (Entry Interface, 400,000 feet or 121.92km altitude:33 hours, 49 minutes GETThese values act as a set of constraints with which the computer calculates the desired trajectory and the details of the burn to achieve it.

tokens: A major component of the spacecraft's primary guidance system is the Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU).
It consists of a gyroscopically-stabilised platform mounted within three gimbals.
As the spacecraft rotates, the platform maintains a constant orientation with respect to the stars.
However, over time, its precise orientation will drift and so Ron has to regularly compare its orientation with the stars so that it can be returned to perfect alignment.
He does this by using program 52 and sighting on two stars that can be seen by the spacecraft's optical system.
This is his first realignment of the flight and he sighted on star 22 Regulus (Alpha Leonis) and star 24 Gienah (Gamma Corvi).
As a check of his sighting accuracy, the computer compared the measured angle between these stars and the actual angle.
The difference between them showed '00000' or 'all balls' on the display, indicating he had marked on them very accurately.
Finally, the computer displayed Noun 93, the angles by which the gimbals Have to be rotated or 'torqued' to restore perfect alignment.
These were -0.037° in X, -0.007° in Y and -0.021° in Z.
The platform was torqued at 001:51:40.

tokens: Central to the spacecraft's navigation is the maintenance of a concept called the state vector.
This is a set of six numbers, applicable at a particular time, and they state two important things; the position and the velocity of the spacecraft.
Each is expressed in three dimensions, hence the six numbers and they are relative to either an Earth-centred or a Moon-centred coordinate system.
Ron is capable of using star/horizon angles to determine the state vector but the primary means of doing so is using radio techniques from the ground.
Having tracked the stack over various ground stations, engineers have derived a revised state vector which needs to be uplinked to a position in memory set aside to accept it.

tokens: Up to the moment of ignition, the stack has been coasting nose first, or Sharp-End Forward (SEF), with its long axis parallel to the ground below.
When the burn starts, it begins to accelerate the stack forward in its orbital motion.
Now the stack is gaining more momentum and its inertia causes its curved flight path around Earth to begin to straighten out so that it begins to pull away from Earth.

This is occurring on the opposite side of Earth from where the Moon will be in a few days time but the far end of the elliptical orbit that is now being shaped will be near the Moon when Apollo 17 gets there.

tokens: Mission planners always allowed for two opportunities for the TLI burn; the first after roughly three hours in orbit and the second after an additional 90 minutes.
In order to be able to achieve the burn at the second opportunity, extra hydrogen fuel was loaded to compensate for the amount that would boil off during that additional orbit.
As a result, there is extra hydrogen available for a first-opportunity burn.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Had they gone to a second-opportunity burn then the J-2 engine would be set to burn at a mixture ratio of 5:1 (5 kg of LOX to 1 kg of hydrogen), producing a thrust of 890 kN.
But for the burn at the first opportunity, the first couple of minutes or so are burned at a 4.5:1 mixture ratio, making it hydrogen-rich and thereby using up the spare fuel.
The thrust was lower with this ratio at 790 kN but perhaps surprisingly, its efficiency was greater with more momentum being added to the stack for each unit of mass consumed in the engine.
The reason for this is that the unburnt hydrogen molecules are easier to accelerate and therefore the exhaust velocity is higher.
Higher exhaust velocity means there is more momentum being trnsferred to the stack per unit mass.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: When approprate, the Propellant Utilisation valve on the J-2 engine is operated by the IU to give a mixture ratio of 5:1, the normal setting for the engine.
The effect for the crew was to feel a surge of power.
With slightly more oxygen available to consume the hydrogen, more energy is produced, increasing the engine's thrust by about 13% while reducing its efficiency (or specific impulse) slightly.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: At the end of the TLI burn, Apollo 17 is on a long ellipse whose apogee is well beyond lunar distance.

This path is set to pass in front of the Moon's leading hemisphere and as the two bodies, the Moon and the spacecraft, gradually come together, the lunar gravity will increasingly take the spacecraft's coasting path away from that original ellipse, essentially taking some momentum out of the orbit.
It will then bend back on itself so that it loops around the lunar far side and head back in the general direction of Earth.
Of course, before then, the crew will carry out a burn at the point of closest approach in order to slow the spacecraft further and enter lunar orbit.
